memories.memory_text: I was summoned by O'Neil that night—cold, direct, no pleasantries. He needed me back, and he wanted me in immediately.
grounded_facts: O'Neil ordered Augustine to get Jimmy McGinty.
O'Neil demanded McGinty be brought 'here. Tonight.'
McGinty was not present in the scene but was directly called for by name.

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. O'NEIL'S BEDROOM - CLOSE ON O'NEIL - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I told O'Neil I'm retired, that he doesn't even have a real team left—just jets flying home to castles—while he's lying there sipping whiskey like this is some kind of negotiation.
grounded_facts: McGinty stated he was retired and pointed out the team had disbanded.

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. O'NEIL'S BEDROOM - CLOSE ON O'NEIL - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: After he kept pushing, I set the terms: I recruit anyone I want, no interference, and absolutely no Notre Dame stories—or I pull his plug myself.
grounded_facts: McGinty agreed to return as head coach only after imposing strict conditions on autonomy and banning Notre Dame stories.

memories.memory_text: I pulled out my own list of players—guys nobody else wanted, most never even made it in the pros—but each had something unique. I told Pilachowski and Banes we weren't rebuilding with cast-offs; we were building something different.
grounded_facts: McGinty interrupts the coordinators' presentation about previously cut players.
He produces his own list of players he has scouted since retiring.
He states the players on his list have something unique to bring to the game.
He declares intent to build a team around these overlooked players.

memories.memory_text: Reese Evans showed up in the doorway, said he was bored and wanted one more hit. I looked at him, knew he meant it, so I nodded to Pilachowski to write his name in as center.
grounded_facts: Reese Evans appears in the doorway during the meeting.
Evans states he is bored, ready to retire, but wants one more chance to play.
He expresses preference for going out with a broken leg over sitting on the bench.
McGinty acknowledges Evans and signals Pilachowski to assign him as center.

memories.memory_text: I offered Shane my flask to cut the chill—he looked half-drowned and stiff from the cold, just flopped onto his boat like a beached fish. He took a drink without hesitation, so I knew he wasn’t completely closed off.
grounded_facts: McGinty offers Shane a flask.
Shane accepts and drinks from it.
Shane has just climbed onto his boat, appearing cold and tired.

memories.memory_text: I told him the truth straight—his problem wasn’t skill, it was courage, that the Sugar Bowl broke something in him. When he looked away, I knew I’d hit the nerve. Then I asked him if he’d rather get hurt playing or spend his life cleaning other men’s boats.
grounded_facts: McGinty identifies Shane's lack of courage as his main issue.
He references Shane's loss in the Sugar Bowl by 45 points.
He challenges Shane to choose between risking injury in football or living a diminished life.
Shane avoids eye contact and stares at the water.

memories.memory_text: I told O'Neil straight—Falco's our quarterback, no debate. He's got the talent, all he needs is confidence, and I'm not bending on that just because the old man's panicking about a safety two days before a game.
grounded_facts: McGinty declares Falco the starting quarterback despite O'Neil's objections.
McGinty believes Falco has natural talent and only lacks self-confidence.
O'Neil expresses concern about not having a safety and mentions a game in five days.
McGinty responds to O'Neil's pressure with firmness and sarcasm.

memories.memory_text: I yanked Shane into the equipment room and demanded to know what the hell that oversized cage on his head was—pointed right at the helmet, called it out: he's a quarterback, not a lineman.
grounded_facts: McGinty entered the equipment room with Shane
McGinty pointed at Shane's helmet
McGinty questioned the equipment man about the helmet
McGinty stated that Shane is a quarterback

memories.memory_text: I held up three fingers right in front of Shane's face to test if he could see straight through that damn cage—he hesitated, said two, then corrected to three, which told me everything.
grounded_facts: McGinty held up three fingers
McGinty asked Shane how many fingers he saw
Shane initially responded with two, then corrected to three

memories.memory_text: I bowed to Mickey Lee and greeted him in Japanese before assigning him to work out at left tackle—he responded in kind, and I could feel Pilachowski staring at me like I’d lost my mind.
grounded_facts: McGinty bows to Mickey Lee and speaks to him in Japanese.
Mickey Lee responds in Japanese and they shake hands.
McGinty assigns Lee to work out at left tackle.

memories.memory_text: I told Pilachowski I met Mickey in Hawaii when he was even bigger, explaining that sumo wrestlers are experts at pushing people—just like pass blocking—knowing he wouldn’t see the logic right away.
grounded_facts: McGinty mentions meeting Mickey Lee in Hawaii when he was larger.
McGinty equates sumo wrestling skills with pass blocking by saying 'he's an expert at pushing people around. That's what pass blocking is, Leo.'
Pilachowski expresses disbelief after McGinty's explanation.

memories.memory_text: I held up my baby girl for the players to see, feeling proud as they admired her—she’s the real win, not any damn game.
grounded_facts: McGinty is at his home hosting a barbecue for the Washington Redskins players.
He holds up his baby daughter for the players to admire.
He kisses the baby and then hands her to his wife.

memories.memory_text: I was standing in the hallway, looking at my trophy case, when Annabelle showed up and started questioning why I came back—like I did it just to prove something with losers.
grounded_facts: McGinty owns a large trophy case filled with football memorabilia.
Annabelle comments that McGinty came out of retirement to see if he could win with losers.
The scene takes place in McGinty's house, implying it is his personal space and the trophies belong to him.

memories.memory_text: I told Shane to stay calm and run the ten planned plays to get a feel for the game, but the moment he got into the huddle, it was pure chaos—shouting, pushing, and then the delay of game penalty. I could hear it all through the headset, but there was nothing I could do from the sideline.
grounded_facts: McGinty communicated with Shane via one-way helmet radio.
He instructed Shane to execute the ten planned plays to establish control.
He heard escalating conflict in the huddle between Andre and Lee.
He was aware of the delay of game penalty called on the offense.

memories.scene_title: 40、EXT. GAME - ANGLE ON SCOREBOARD - SECOND QUARTER
memories.memory_text: I told Shane to pick up his pace on the roll and see the big picture—pump and fake, pump and fake—after he panicked and threw the ball out of bounds when Lamont was open in the flat.
grounded_facts: McGinty gave verbal feedback to Shane from the sidelines after Shane made a poor throw.
McGinty pointed out that Lamont was open in the flat.
McGinty instructed Shane to improve his rolling pace and use pump fakes to create better field awareness.

memories.memory_text: I told Shane to look for Murray over the middle, that he's a big, tough kid with good hands—he needed to hear it quiet and straight, no bullshit.
grounded_facts: McGinty bends down to Shane in the locker room during halftime.
McGinty speaks quietly to Shane about targeting Murray.
McGinty describes Murray as 'a big, tough kid with good hands.'

memories.memory_text: I reminded Shane he's a thoroughbred out there running against a bunch of nags—he smiled through the pain, so he heard me.
grounded_facts: McGinty tells Shane he is a 'thoroughbred' and the others are 'nags'.
Shane smiles through his pain after McGinty's statement.
The statement occurs in the same locker room interaction during halftime.

memories.memory_text: I told Shane over the headset that half his job was getting the huddle in the right mindset — make them feel it, make it real. Then I watched him stop short, make them remember where they came from, and pull them back in.
grounded_facts: McGinty delivered a voiceover instruction to Shane about the importance of setting the huddle's mindset.
He emphasized using imagination to influence the team's psychological state.
He observed Shane pause before the huddle, then lead a moment of reflection that visibly affected the players.
McGinty saw Shane successfully execute a play afterward and acknowledged it with a smile.

memories.memory_text: I told Shane over the headset, 'Don't try to bullshit me, pal. You didn't want it.' He looked down like he always does when he can't face the truth.
grounded_facts: McGinty speaks to Shane via the helmet radio after Shane fails to execute the handoff properly
McGinty accuses Shane of not wanting the ball in a critical moment
Shane responds by looking down
indicating shame or submission

memories.memory_text: I gave the call for 'waggle right, drag hook' and made sure Shane heard it—no room for confusion, not with seconds left.
grounded_facts: McGinty relays a specific play call—'waggle right
drag hook'—to Shane through the helmet radio
The instruction is given in a high-pressure moment with eight seconds remaining
Shane acknowledges the call with a nod

memories.memory_text: I called a dinner meeting with the whole team—time to get these guys back in line, see who’s really committed now that some regulars are crossing the picket line.
grounded_facts: McGinty called a dinner meeting with the whole team.
Some regular players are starting to cross the picket line.

memories.memory_text: I tapped my glass at the head of the table and told the team that union players want to come back, but Mister O'Neil and I decided to ban them and keep the replacement players.
grounded_facts: McGinty taps his glass to get attention at the dinner.
McGinty announces that union players are trying to return.
McGinty states that he and Mister O'Neil have decided to ban striking players.
McGinty affirms the replacement players will continue playing.
The announcement is followed by cheers from the replacement players.

memories.memory_text: Right after the room started celebrating, the door burst open and a reporter shoved a microphone in my face, asking about the game plan for New York—like we hadn’t just made history in here.
grounded_facts: A reporter and cameraman enter the private room uninvited.
The reporter interrupts by shoving a microphone at McGinty.
The reporter asks about the strategy against New York.
The intrusion happens immediately after the announcement and the team's celebration.

memories.memory_text: I saw Shane get flattened and buried under Morris—guy used his helmet like a step to get up. I keyed the mike and asked if he wanted a time-out. Didn't matter if he said yes or no. I was checking on him.
grounded_facts: McGinty observed Shane being hit hard and having Morris land on him.
Morris placed a hand on Shane's helmet to push himself up, pressing the face guard into the turf.
McGinty spoke into his microphone and offered a time-out, saying, 'You want a time-out? That looks like it hurt.'
Shane did not verbally respond to the offer but got up and continued playing.

memories.memory_text: I'm putting together a team of replacements, and yeah, one of them's a felon—but I don't care, as long as he can play.
grounded_facts: McGinty is coaching a team composed entirely of replacement players.
There is at least one known felon on the team he is assembling.
The information about the felon is publicly known and mentioned in a news report.

memories.memory_text: I told O'Neil Falco could play under house arrest—kept under control but still on the field, just like I said. Set up in the stadium, no loose ends.
grounded_facts: McGinty states that Falco will be under house arrest but still able to play due to a work release program.
McGinty proposes housing Falco in the stadium and making arrangements for him to stay there during house arrest.

memories.memory_text: I made it clear to O'Neil—I want Falco kept as Martel's backup after the strike. Didn't matter what he wanted; the union rules were in play, and I used that to lock it in.
grounded_facts: McGinty explicitly states his intention to keep Falco as Martel's backup after the strike ends.
McGinty reminds O'Neil that union players must be taken back after the strike due to the collective bargaining agreement, asserting strategic control over team composition.

memories.memory_text: I pulled Shane aside and told him it was official—he was staying on after the strike. He smiled through the pain, and I told him to keep it quiet until after the game.
grounded_facts: McGinty pulled Shane aside in the locker room.
McGinty informed Shane that he was officially staying on the team after the strike.
Shane smiled despite appearing to be in physical discomfort.

memories.memory_text: I stood in the middle of the locker room and told the team this wasn't about contracts or money—it was about proving what heart really means, and that with no tomorrow, they were dangerous.
grounded_facts: McGinty delivered a speech in the center of the locker room.
The speech emphasized that sports are about rising to the occasion, not contracts or commercial interests.
McGinty framed the game as a final opportunity to demonstrate heart, noting the players had 'no tomorrow,' making them dangerous.

memories.memory_text: I watched Shane get crushed by Butler again and saw him gasp when he got up — something was wrong, so I keyed the mic and asked, 'What's the matter with your arm? Are you alright?'
grounded_facts: McGinty observes Shane being hit hard by Butler and struggling to get up.
McGinty sees Shane grimace and gasp in pain after the play.
McGinty uses the headset to ask Shane about his condition.

memories.memory_text: I walked into the locker room and told them the strike was over—this half was all they had left to keep from being humiliated. I told them their self-respect was on the line, not the scoreboard.
grounded_facts: McGinty entered the middle of the locker room during a moment of physical and emotional exhaustion among the players.
He announced that the strike was officially over and broadcasted on TV.
He emphasized that the team still had one half to play and that their self-respect depended on how they performed, regardless of the current score.
He referred to the final half as their 'swan song' and challenged them to decide how they would end it.
After delivering the message, he left the room without waiting for a response.

memories.memory_text: I saw Shane leaning against the wall, broken after the play, and when our eyes met, I turned my back and walked away.
grounded_facts: McGinty exits the locker room and makes eye contact with Shane.
McGinty turns his back on Shane and walks away without speaking.

memories.memory_text: I smiled after the play succeeded—Shane threw that bullet downfield just right, and Murray caught it clean in the end zone. The crowd roared, and when Shane walked up, I shook his hand like a man who’d earned it.
grounded_facts: McGinty is described as 'all smiles' after the Redskins win.
Shane walks up to McGinty and they shake hands.

memories.memory_text: I saw Eddie Martel slip and grab his knee on the stairs, screaming—just after he stormed off. Didn’t say a word, but I knew that moment changed everything.
grounded_facts: Eddie Martel jumps up from his seat, turns quickly, slips awkwardly, falls, grabs his knee, and screams.

memories.memory_text: They still call it 'The Big O'—my name echoing in that stadium, even now, even with me dying. I can't breathe easy, but damn, at least they remember.
grounded_facts: The stadium is commonly known as 'The Big O,' a nickname derived from Edward Francis O'Neil's name.
The scene takes place at the home stadium of the Washington Redskins, where O'Neil's legacy is symbolically present through the nickname.
O'Neil is aware of his impending death and has difficulty breathing, consistent with his established physical condition.

memories.memory_text: I was watching the game with the sound off when I realized they lost — I couldn't stop the wail, it just ripped out of me, raw and loud, like something tore loose inside my chest.
grounded_facts: O'Neil is in bed, watching the Redskins game with the sound off.
He emits a loud, piercing wail upon realizing the team lost.
The emotional reaction is immediate and overwhelming.

memories.memory_text: After he stuck me with that needle, I could feel the fog rolling in, but I still grabbed the phone and screamed into it — 'What the fuck was that?!' — I had to make them hear me, even if my body was shutting down.
grounded_facts: O'Neil is injected with a sedative by the nurse.
He speaks to the coach on the phone while under the influence of the sedative.
He uses profanity and expresses rage toward the coach.

memories.memory_text: I remember yelling at Tildon on the phone—'Bullshit he slipped!'—and telling him I could've scored even with one foot in the grave. Then I screamed 'You god damn wimp! You're fired! Get out!' and threw the phone down.
grounded_facts: O'Neil shouted 'Bullshit he slipped!' during a phone call with Coach Tildon.
He expressed disbelief at Tildon's excuse and claimed he could have scored despite his poor health.
He called Tildon a 'god damn wimp' and terminated his position by yelling 'You're fired! Get out! Get out! Get out!'
He physically threw the phone after ending the call.

memories.memory_text: Right after firing Tildon, I gasped for air, sucked hard on the oxygen, and told Augustine to get Jimmy McGinty—here, tonight. I needed him now.
grounded_facts: O'Neil used supplemental oxygen immediately after the phone call, adjusting it with visible effort.
He explicitly demanded 'Get me Jimmy McGinty. Get him here. Tonight.'
The request followed the firing and was delivered while struggling to breathe.

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. O'NEIL'S BEDROOM - CLOSE ON O'NEIL - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I took the oxygen mask off just to taste that whiskey McGinty poured me, and when I looked at him, I told him straight — I'm dying, Jimmy. I could see he didn't want to believe it, but I needed him to hear it.
grounded_facts: O'Neil removes his oxygen mask to drink whiskey.
O'Neil tells McGinty 'I'm dying, Jimmy' while looking at him.
McGinty initially dismisses the claim, saying O'Neil has 'been dying for twenty years.'

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. O'NEIL'S BEDROOM - CLOSE ON O'NEIL - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I called him a pussy and told him to come back as head coach — I'd already fired Tilden — and after all his excuses, I laid it on him: help me bring a winner back to D.C. before I'm gone by Superbowl Sunday.
grounded_facts: O'Neil calls McGinty a 'pussy' and demands he return as head coach.
O'Neil states he fired Coach Tilden earlier that day.
O'Neil reveals his doctor's prognosis that he will die by Superbowl Sunday.
O'Neil pleads with McGinty to help bring a winning team back to Washington, D.C.

memories.memory_text: I watched that kid throw the ball through the damn ring, and all I could think was, 'This is what you're building an offense around?'
grounded_facts: O'Neil observes Shane Falco throwing a football through a rubber ring on the goalpost.
O'Neil expresses skepticism about building an offense around Falco.

memories.memory_text: I remember yelling at Jimmy McGinty about Footsteps Falco—what kind of quarterback has the happiest feet I've ever seen? I asked him to build a team with balls, not some jittery kid who dances more than he throws.
grounded_facts: O'Neil expressed anger and disbelief at McGinty's choice of Footsteps Falco as quarterback.
O'Neil mocked Falco's playing style by referencing his 'happiest feet' and contrasted it with his demand for a tough, bold team.
The interaction occurred while O'Neil was seated on the fifty-yard line, visibly struggling with his breathing.

memories.memory_text: I told McGinty I'd already called the Governor of Maryland—didn't say how, didn't say why, but I made it clear I was moving on the safety problem myself, because he wasn't doing shit.
grounded_facts: O'Neil revealed he contacted the Governor of Maryland to address the lack of a safety.
He presented the call as a decisive action taken independently, asserting control over the situation.
McGinty responded sarcastically, implying skepticism about the Governor actually playing safety.

memories.memory_text: I watched that van roll in and saw Earl step out in cuffs, that wild look in his eyes like he hadn't eaten in days—hell, like he *was* the predator, not the prey.
grounded_facts: A van arrived at the stadium.
Earl Wilkinson exited the van wearing handcuffs and jail clothes.
A Maryland state trooper removed Earl's cuffs.
Earl had a bearded, muscular, and dangerous appearance.
Earl's expression was intense, described as 'hungry'.

memories.memory_text: I lunged for that tequila bottle—just needed a drink, something to burn through this damn pain—but Augustine ripped it out of my hands like I was some junkie. Felt like everyone saw it.
grounded_facts: O'Neil is in a wheelchair and being pushed by Augustine.
O'Neil suddenly lunges for a bottle of tequila at the barbecue.
Augustine physically intervenes and removes the bottle from O'Neil's grasp.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't even get a word in—Shane walked into that huddle and got thrown around like a damn ragdoll while those two tore each other apart. All I could do was watch from the sideline, breathing hard, knowing this team was falling apart right in front of me.
grounded_facts: O'Neil observed the conflict in the huddle from the sideline
Shane entered the huddle and was knocked down during the physical confrontation between Andre and Lee
O'Neil experienced physical discomfort, likely related to breathing, while witnessing the incident

memories.scene_title: 40、EXT. GAME - ANGLE ON SCOREBOARD - SECOND QUARTER
memories.memory_text: I don't feel good. And then I just lost it—sixteen eggs all over the field. I couldn't hold it back, not with everything burning in my gut and my lungs screaming for air.
grounded_facts: Lee says 'I don't feel good' before vomiting.
Lee vomits sixteen eggs during the huddle.
Lee is physically exhausted, as shown by heavy breathing and visible distress.

memories.memory_text: I pounded on Augustine, I felt alive again, like the fire was back in my chest—this team actually did it.
grounded_facts: O'Neil is present on the field during the climactic victory.
He physically reacts by pounding on Augustine.
His demeanor shifts, appearing 'ten years younger' while watching the play unfold.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't even get my damn coat on by myself—had to let Annabelle help me. I told her thank you, and that she saved my life, but she brushed it off like it was nothing.
grounded_facts: Shane is in pain and unable to put on his coat without assistance.
Annabelle helps Shane with his coat.
Shane expresses gratitude and says Annabelle saved his life.

memories.memory_text: I mentioned how the pill worked like a charm, but Annabelle shot back that it was Vicodin and warned me it's habit-forming—like she was already expecting me to get hooked.
grounded_facts: Shane comments that the pill 'sure worked'.
Annabelle identifies the pill as Vicodin and warns about its addictive nature.
Shane shows early reliance on painkillers.

memories.memory_text: I whispered to Cochran, desperate, offering a thousand dollars for just one bite of his steak while Augustine was feeding me pureed asparagus like I was a goddamn infant.
grounded_facts: O'Neil is being fed pureed asparagus by Augustine
O'Neil whispers to Cochran and offers $1,000 for a bite of Cochran's steak
Other players are eating whole foods like steak and lobster while O'Neil cannot

memories.memory_text: I told Jimmy to go to hell with the union rules — I'm dying, I don't care if they sue me or shut the stadium down. Let 'em try.
grounded_facts: O'Neil refuses to reinstate union players after the strike, defying the collective bargaining agreement.
He explicitly states 'The hell I do!' in response to being told he must take the union players back.
He invokes his terminal illness as justification, saying 'I'm dying!'

memories.memory_text: I made Jimmy promise to beat Dallas for me — the whole country will be watching, and if they win, I'll go quietly. I can't die without seeing that.
grounded_facts: O'Neil insists that McGinty beat Dallas, linking it to national attention and personal closure.
He says, 'you beat Dallas for me, and then I'll go gently into that night,' directly tying the game's outcome to his emotional peace.
He emphasizes the symbolic victory over wealthy players, wanting Dallas humiliated.

memories.memory_text: I watched Lindell sweating next to that hard-eyed suit Matheson, spouting unity while the guy demanded the players cave outright—no changes, just surrender.
grounded_facts: O'Neil observes Jerome Lindell and Matheson together in the parking lot during a press moment.
Matheson states the owners' only condition is that players return to work without contract changes.
Lindell appears uncomfortable but maintains a smile despite the tension.

memories.memory_text: I was scraping algae off the boat, muck floating past me, when I saw something golden glinting in the mud below—among the beer cans and trash, a broken trophy half-buried in the silt.
grounded_facts: Shane was cleaning the underside of a sailboat with a spatula.
He noticed a golden object on the ocean floor while working.
The object was surrounded by trash and partially buried in mud.

memories.memory_text: I held the bronze football in my hands, gripped the seams like old habit, and suddenly I was calling signals—bubbling out 'Blue!'—dropping back, faking, dodging an imaginary blitz, rolling left, and throwing the trophy like a real pass before signaling touchdown.
grounded_facts: Shane picked up the broken trophy and examined it closely.
He gripped it along the seams as if it were a real football.
He performed a sequence mimicking quarterback mechanics: play call, dropback, play action, evasion, and release.
He threw the trophy forward and made a touchdown celebration.

memories.memory_text: McGinty offered me the Redskins quarterback job while I was still dripping wet from the dinghy, sitting there in my patched wetsuit, and I could feel the weight of that flask in my hand as I tried to process what he was saying.
grounded_facts: Shane was wearing a patched wetsuit and had just climbed out of a dinghy onto his boat.
McGinty offered Shane the quarterback position for the Redskins during their conversation.
McGinty handed Shane a flask, which Shane took and drank from.

memories.memory_text: When McGinty said my biggest problem was courage and that the Sugar Bowl broke me, I looked away because I couldn't face him—just stared at the water, feeling the silence press in, not knowing how to answer.
grounded_facts: McGinty stated that Shane's biggest problem was courage and referenced the Sugar Bowl as a breaking point.
There was a beat where Shane looked away after McGinty's comment about courage.
Shane did not respond immediately and instead remained silent, staring at the water.

memories.memory_text: I shook Lou Pacifico’s hand, introduced myself, and then he told me he lost a lot of money on my Sugar Bowl disaster—said I got my butt kicked. I could feel the weight of that game again, right there on the bus.
grounded_facts: Shane Falco introduced himself to Lou Pacifico on the bus.
Lou Pacifico acknowledged Shane by name and referenced losing money on the Sugar Bowl game.
Lou commented that Shane 'got his butt kicked' during the Sugar Bowl.
The interaction occurred while eggs were being thrown at the bus by striking players.

memories.memory_text: I could barely see through the helmet they gave me—too much metal, like a lineman’s cage—and when McGinty held up his fingers, I had to hesitate before getting the number right. I said I could see, but even I knew it wasn’t true.
grounded_facts: Shane is wearing a linebacker's helmet with an exaggerated face guard that obstructs his vision.
McGinty questions the helmet because Shane is a quarterback and needs clear visibility.
Sid claims Shane told him he was a linebacker.
McGinty tests Shane's vision by holding up three fingers.
Shane initially says two, then corrects to three.

memories.memory_text: I was just trying to get dressed after the game, sitting there in the locker room, when reporters swarmed me with mics in my face—asking where I’ve been, what I’ve been doing. I told them I’ve been in the aquatic business, not sure they bought it, but that’s all I wanted to say.
grounded_facts: Shane is in the locker room, partially undressed, sitting on a bench.
Reporters are surrounding him with microphones and cameras.
He responds to questions by saying he's been involved in the 'aquatic business'.
He gives a minimal, vague response to deflect further inquiry.

memories.memory_text: I walked into the huddle and tried to calm Andre and Lee down, but they ignored me—shoving each other, yelling—while I stood there, their captain, and no one listened.
grounded_facts: Shane enters the huddle while Andre and Lee are arguing.
Shane asks, 'Hold on, what's the problem here?'
Andre and Lee continue pushing each other and ignore Shane.
Jamal intervenes and tells Lee not to mess with his brother.
Shane attempts to get between the teammates during the scuffle.

memories.memory_text: I got knocked down when Andre spun into me and then Jamal's wild punch hit me in the back of the helmet—I hit the ground hard, dazed, and the noise just blurred into silence for a second.
grounded_facts: Lee punches Andre through his face guard.
Andre is knocked into Shane, spinning him around.
Jamal attempts to punch Lee but misses and hits Shane in the back of the helmet.
Shane goes down hard after being struck.

memories.memory_text: I dropped back into the shotgun, felt the blitz coming, rolled left then shifted right — I saw Franklin open near the goal line and cocked my arm, but they were all over me before I could make the throw.
grounded_facts: Shane drops into the shotgun formation.
Shane rolls left, then shifts and rolls right to avoid pressure.
Shane sees Franklin wide open near the goal line.
Shane begins to throw but is hit immediately after releasing the ball.

memories.memory_text: I'm on the ground, can't move, everything's muffled — I hear Todd, Rod, and Bob screaming, and then Summerall's voice cuts through: 'Falco is intercepted' — and I know it's bad, but I also know the clock's still running.
grounded_facts: Shane is down on the field and not moving after the hit.
Teammates Todd, Rod, and Bob scream in despair.
Announcer Summerall states 'Falco is intercepted.'
The clock shows forty-eight seconds remaining.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing at my door with two women, one in red and one in blue, and saying 'Oooooh. Bookends,' feeling drunk and reckless, just wanting to disappear into the night with them.
grounded_facts: Shane is in the hallway with the Woman In Red and then the Woman In Blue joins them.
Shane fumbles for his key and makes a comment about 'bookends' upon seeing both women.
He refers to the women as 'my guests' when confronted by Annabelle.
He expresses a desire to be taken advantage of by saying, 'That's what I was hoping.'

memories.memory_text: I was scrambling, not even thinking about throwing—just trying to survive those linemen coming at me, and then Morris hit me, drove me into the ground and whispered in my ear like it was personal.
grounded_facts: Shane was running from three defensive linemen and not looking to pass.
Shane was tackled by Hank Morris, who landed on top of him.
Hank Morris said 'Hello, again!' while in Shane's ear after the tackle.

memories.scene_title: 60、INT. CONTROL BOOTH - ON SUMMERALL AND MADDEN - DAY
memories.memory_text: I can feel every hit stacking up, especially after Hank Morris took me down again—third time now, maybe the sixth this game—I don’t need the announcers to tell me I’m getting torn apart, but I’ve still got to get up and do it again.
grounded_facts: Shane Falco was sacked multiple times, including a third sack by Hank Morris.
The sacks occurred during a defensive-heavy game with the Giants leading 10-3.
Falco remained in the game despite accumulating physical punishment.

memories.memory_text: I got up with turf stuck in my face guard, spitting dirt, so angry I could taste blood—I couldn’t let him break me again.
grounded_facts: Shane had turf stuck in his face guard after being tackled
He pulled out the dirt and grass, revealing his anger
He initiated a huddle immediately after rising

memories.memory_text: I told Lee and Cochran to let Morris through untouched, then threw the ball straight at his face—saw it stick in his mask like a dart and blind him.
grounded_facts: Shane instructed Lee and Cochran to let Morris pass without contact
He executed a three-step drop from shotgun formation
He threw the ball directly at Morris's face, hitting his face guard
The ball became lodged in Morris's helmet, temporarily blinding him

memories.memory_text: I tackled Danny Bateman to stop him from running around after the onside kick recovery—he was burning clock, and we needed every second for a final play.
grounded_facts: Shane tackled Bateman after he gained eight yards and turned back upfield
Shane yelled 'No! Danny, go down!' while chasing him
The clock showed eighteen seconds remaining after Bateman was tackled

memories.memory_text: I held the ball for Pacifico’s 65-yard field goal attempt, looking at his clean uniform while mine was covered in blood and dirt—then snapped it clean under pressure.
grounded_facts: Shane set up to receive the snap for the field goal
Pacifico smoked a cigarette before the kick and flicked it away
Shane commented on Pacifico's clean uniform before the snap
The snap was perfect and the kick was successful

memories.memory_text: I stood at her door and finally said it — I don’t want to be alone. It came out raw, like I was admitting something I’d been fighting for a long time.
grounded_facts: Shane says 'I don't want to be alone' to Annabelle at her apartment door.
The statement follows a pause where Shane struggles to speak.

memories.memory_text: I told her I was scared of her — the words just came out — and then she just stood there, thinking about it, like she was weighing what that meant.
grounded_facts: Shane says 'I'm kind of scared of you' to Annabelle.
Annabelle does not react immediately but takes in the statement and thinks about it.

memories.memory_text: I told Annabelle about the Sugar Bowl, how I was sacked eleven times, threw six interceptions, set two records no one wants — I could still feel the hits, the silence after each turnover.
grounded_facts: Shane mentions being sacked eleven times and throwing six interceptions in the Sugar Bowl. Shane recalls setting two Sugar Bowl records related to negative outcomes. The memory is shared in a vulnerable
post-intimate moment. Shane connects the loss to public humiliation and personal failure.

memories.memory_text: I admitted I lost my nerve, that I got so afraid of failing in the big moments that I started believing I didn’t deserve to win at anything — not even love.
grounded_facts: Shane states he lost his nerve and edge needed to play. He explicitly links fear of failure in games to a broader belief in personal unworthiness. Shane connects his athletic collapse to feeling undeserving in love. Annabelle responds by saying he’s winning her
grounding the emotional stakes.

memories.memory_text: McGinty told O'Neil he wanted to keep me on after the strike, as Martel's backup—that was the first time I knew I’d earned a real spot on the team.
grounded_facts: McGinty states his intention to keep Shane Falco on the team after the strike.
Falco is to be kept as the backup quarterback behind Martel.

memories.memory_text: I turned around and saw Eddie Martel sitting on my bed, two Cowboys linemen by the window — I barely got out a 'What's this?' before they grabbed me when I tried to run.
grounded_facts: Shane entered his room and saw Martel on his bed with two linemen present.
Shane attempted to flee but was physically restrained by the linemen.
Martel initiated the confrontation with a veiled threat about the upcoming game.

memories.memory_text: After he kicked me in the ribs twice and I was on the floor gasping, Martel made me say out loud that I wouldn't go out with Annabelle anymore — I could barely speak, but he made me say it.
grounded_facts: Martel kicked Shane in the side, causing visible pain and difficulty breathing.
Martel demanded Shane verbally promise to stop seeing Annabelle.
Shane, in pain and on the floor, complied with the demand under duress.

memories.memory_text: I remember stepping onto the field knowing every Cowboy was back—every single one had crossed the line, and now we, the scabs, had to face the full force of America's Team.
grounded_facts: Shane Falco is playing against the Dallas Cowboys who have all returned to play.
The opposing team is referred to as the 'mighty Dallas Cowboys' and 'America's team'.
Falco is part of a group described as 'strike players' or 'Scabskins'.
The game takes place in Washington D.C. at a venue referred to as 'the Big O'.
The context includes a labor strike that is widely seen as ineffective.

memories.memory_text: I smiled when Coach McGinty told me I was staying on after the strike, even though my body was aching—I could feel the weight of it, but also the relief.
grounded_facts: Coach McGinty informs Shane that he will remain on the team after the strike.
Shane smiles in spite of physical pain.
The conversation happens privately before McGinty addresses the full team.

memories.memory_text: I was rolling left, Butler right on my heels, and I had to get rid of it—threw a weak one to Murray that fell short. Next thing I know, I'm on the ground, crushed, trying to breathe.
grounded_facts: Shane was under pressure while rolling left with Butler pursuing.
He threw an incomplete pass to Murray.
He was hit hard by Butler immediately after releasing the ball.

memories.memory_text: Butler leaned on my side as I got up, asking 'How's that side feel?'—I said 'Like Christmas morning,' but I could barely hide the pain, gasping as I stood.
grounded_facts: Butler made physical contact with Shane's injured side after a play.
Butler verbally taunted Shane with 'How's that side feel?'
Shane responded through gritted teeth and visibly struggled with pain.

memories.memory_text: I could feel my side tearing with every step, but I kept running toward the end zone—only the safety left, and when he came up, I slid just like Eddie Martel did, and then the gun went off.
grounded_facts: Shane is in physical pain while running toward the end zone
He sees only a safety between him and scoring
He performs a sliding move similar to Eddie Martel's
The half ends immediately after the slide, signaled by the gun

memories.memory_text: After the slide, I saw trainers running onto the field, and when I turned back, Lamont was on the ground clutching his knee, rolling in pain.
grounded_facts: Shane observes trainers rushing onto the field
He turns and sees Lamont holding his knee and rolling on the ground
Lamont is in visible pain after making a block

memories.memory_text: I stopped the paramedics to tell Roland I was sorry and that his block was great, but I couldn't even look him in the eye when he asked why I didn't score.
grounded_facts: Shane speaks to Roland Lamont on the stretcher.
Shane says 'I'm sorry, Roland' and 'That was a great block'.
Roland asks if Shane slipped, and Shane nods in response.
Shane feels guilt and avoids eye contact.

memories.memory_text: After Roland was wheeled away, McGinty came out and just looked at me for a second—then turned his back and walked off without saying a word.
grounded_facts: McGinty exits the locker room after Roland is taken away.
Shane and McGinty lock eyes briefly.
McGinty turns away from Shane and walks off silently.
Shane is left alone, slumped against the wall.

memories.memory_text: I walked in and Andre asked if it was true I was staying, and I told him yes — the silence after that was heavy, but I had to be straight with them.
grounded_facts: Shane confirms to Andre that he is staying with the team
The locker room is silent after his confirmation
Shane acknowledges the tension and distrust in the room

memories.memory_text: I had Sid help me take off my jersey so they could see the bruises, and I asked someone to tape me up — I wanted them to know I was all in.
grounded_facts: Shane removes his jersey with Sid's help
His bruised side is visible to the team
He requests to be taped up, signaling physical sacrifice and commitment

memories.memory_text: I ran over to Annabelle, told her I owed her an apology and that she gives me strength just by being there—I kissed her right then, not caring who saw.
grounded_facts: Shane approaches Annabelle after seeing her cheer
Shane says he owes Annabelle an apology and thanks her for believing in him
Shane tells Annabelle she gives him strength just by looking at her
Shane kisses Annabelle deeply
initiating physical contact in front of the crowd

memories.memory_text: After kissing her, I looked up and saw Eddie Martel in the stands—cold, unimpressed—so I flipped him the finger, not holding back.
grounded_facts: Shane breaks the kiss and looks toward the stands
Shane sees Eddie Martel watching
Shane gives Eddie Martel the middle finger as a defiant gesture
The crowd reacts with another
Wooooo!
to the gesture

memories.memory_text: I threw the ball to Franklin in the end zone just as Butler crushed me — I don’t remember hitting the ground, but I felt the hit and heard my own voice mumbling something stupid while I was out.
grounded_facts: Shane Falco threw a touchdown pass to Franklin while being hit by Butler.
He was decked during the throw and ended up on his back, unconscious.
He was mumbling incoherently ('Put your tits on my head') as trainers attended to him.
Teammates carried him off the field after the play.
